sections.content:

You are still on the same level.

Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'RIGHT': 'Moves avatar +3 cols right, charcoal also +3 cols right (VERIFIED steps 1-4)', 'LEFT': 'Moves avatar -3 cols left, charcoal also -3 cols left (VERIFIED steps 5-6)', 'DOWN': 'Moves avatar +3 rows down, charcoal also +3 rows down (VERIFIED steps 7-10)', 'UP': 'Moves avatar -3 rows up, charcoal also -3 rows up (HYPOTHESIS)'}
- Object taxonomy: {'black_with_dots': 'Player avatar - black 6x6 shape with 5 white dot features, moves with arrow keys', 'charcoal_shape': 'Charcoal object that moves same direction as avatar by 3 pixels, initially at offset', 'yellow_shape': 'Target object at (45,51), 45 pixels, L-shaped, 6x6', 'sky_blue_strip': 'Vertical divider at cols 30-32, changes shape as objects cross it'}
- HUD map: {'bottom_row': 'Black strip at row 63, border', 'right_col': 'Yellow strip at col 63, border'}
Current level:
- World model: Avatar overshot target and merged with bottom black strip (node 7, 103 pixels, rows 54-63, cols 0-62). Target yellow at (45,51). Avatar likely stuck at bottom edge.
- Goal model: Move avatar to overlap with yellow target at (45,51)
- Recent findings: ['Avatar moved too far down and merged with bottom border', 'Need to go UP to reach target row 45']
- Open questions: ['What completes the level - overlap with target?', 'Can avatar escape bottom border merge?', 'Does SPACE or MOUSE do something special?']
- Plan: Try UP moves to get avatar back to target row. If UP doesn't work, try SPACE or MOUSE.
- Strategy log: ['Overshot target by going DOWN 7 + RIGHT 6 from (33,42) to (54,60), then corrected with UP 3 + LEFT 3']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 48 actions; a strong score needs about 32 or fewer. You are 1.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the image, the target (yellow L-shape) is still to the right of the avatar. My hypothesis that "target is opposite to last move" doesn't seem right because after RIGHT, the target should be to the LEFT, but it's still on the right.
Let me reconsider. Looking at the image:
- Avatar (black with dots) is at the bottom right
- Yellow L-shape is above and to the left of the avatar
- Charcoal L-shape is at the bottom left
Wait, I need to look more carefully. The yellow shape appears to be positioned above the avatar, not to the right. Let me check the segmentation data:
- Y: top=(45,51), pixels=27 - this is the target
- B: top=(48,48), pixels=40 - this is the avatar
So the target is at row 45, col 51 and the avatar is at row 48, col 48. The target is above and slightly to the right of the avatar.
Hmm, let me reconsider the pattern. Let me track the positions more carefully:
- After LEFT: avatar at (48,48), target at (45,51) - target is above-right of avatar
- After RIGHT: avatar at (48,51), target at (45,51) - target is directly above avatar
Wait, the target stayed at (45,51) but the avatar moved from (48,48) to (48,51). So the target didn't move! It's fixed at (45,51)!
But earlier I thought the target moved after DOWN. Let me reconsider... Maybe the target doesn't move at all, and what I thought was the target moving was actually just the avatar moving relative to a fixed target.
Let me check the history more carefully. The target has been at (45,51) the whole time! The "orbiting" I observed was just the avatar moving around.
So the goal is probably to get the avatar to overlap with the target at (45,51). The avatar is currently at (48,51), so I need to move UP by 3 rows to get to row 45.
Let me try UP!

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Target is FIXED at (45,51)! It doesn't move.
# Avatar is at (48,51), need to move UP to (45,51)
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg:
    if n['pixels'] > 10 and n['color'] in ['B', 'c', 'Y']:
        b = n['boundary']
        r, c = min([p[0] for p in b]), min([p[1] for p in b])
        print(f"{n['color']}: top=({r},{c}), pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Avatar at (48,51), target at (45,51) - just need UP
action(['UP'])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"RIGHT": "Moves avatar +3 cols right", "LEFT": "Moves avatar -3 cols left", "DOWN": "Moves avatar +3 rows down", "UP": "Moves avatar -3 rows up", "SPACE": "No effect yet", "MOUSE": "No effect (VERIFIED)"}, "object_taxonomy": {"black_with_dots": "Player avatar", "charcoal_shape": "Moves with avatar", "yellow_shape": "Fixed target at (45,51)"}, "hud_map": {"bottom_row": "Black strip", "right_col": "Yellow strip"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": ""}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Target is FIXED at (45,51), avatar at (48,51)", "goal_model": "Move avatar to overlap target at (45,51)", "recent_findings": ["Target doesn't move, it's fixed!"], "open_questions": ["Does overlapping complete the level?"], "current_plan": "Move UP to reach target row 45", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["ACTION7", "MOUSE on target", "SPACE when target not adjacent"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

Y: top=(45,51), pixels=27
Y: top=(45,63), pixels=19
c: top=(48,3), pixels=45

B: top=(63,0), pixels=63
